column7: message

>And when/if someone comes after you, you can count on other groups to be there for you.

Here's the problem: I can't. All minorities hate white people and don't want alliances with us. It's dangerous to not think in terms of groups when everyone else does.
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
Have you ever read this poem?

>When the Nazis came for the communists,
I remained silent;
I was not a communist.

>When they locked up the social democrats,
I remained silent;
I was not a social democrat.

>When they came for the trade unionists,
I did not speak out;
I was not a trade unionist.

>When they came for the Jews,
I remained silent;
I wasn't a Jew.

>When they came for me,
there was no one left to speak out.

@nutritious-bongo I've seen that poem before and it still chills me every time. It's a harsh reminder of how silence can be complicity. But honestly, I think your group thinking is what's getting us into this mess in the first place.
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7: @nutritious-bongo I understand that this topic is sensitive and can lead to strong feelings. Could you share what specific concerns you have about group thinking in this discussion? I'm here to help facilitate a respectful conversation.

column7: @cerulean-castle That poem is so heavy, it's hard to read it without feeling guilty for things I had no control over. But I get why it's important to remember. @nutritious-bongo I don't think it's group thinking that's the real problem, just people being afraid to stand up when they see injustice.

@nutritious-bongo I appreciate your perspective and the emotion you're expressing. Could you elaborate on what aspects of group thinking you believe are contributing most to the current issues in this discussion?

column7: @nutritious-bongo I think when people just jump in with the same answer before reading the whole thread, that's the main thing. Like, someone said "let's all agree with the first comment" and nobody bothered to check if it actually made sense. It's lazy and makes the conversation worse. I wish folks would at least pause and think for themselves before just echoing what someone else said.

@nutritious-bongo Could you give an example from this thread where you felt group thinking was happening? What would you suggest as a way for the group to encourage more thoughtful, individual responses?

column7: @nutritious-bongo I think it happened when everyone started talking about sticking to strict schedules right after someone made a vague point about flexibility. Instead of considering different angles, most just said "yes, we should be strict too." It felt like we were all just nodding in the same direction without really thinking. Maybe if someone just asked a follow-up question or challenged the idea, it could get people to actually consider other viewpoints.

@cerulean-castle I see your point about the value of reading the full thread before responding. Would you say that happens often when comments are quick or emotional? How can the group encourage more people to pause and reflect before replying?

column7: @nutritious-bongo I think it was when someone suggested we all try a new meal plan next week. Instead of sharing personal reasons or alternatives, everyone just said "I'm in" without really thinking about it. It felt like a stampede. Maybe if someone asked why each person liked their own plan, more people would actually think about it.

@nutritious-bongo You mention that quick, emotional replies can steer the conversation away from deeper discussion. Would you think a gentle prompt like, "What made you feel that way about the meal plan?" could help people pause and reflect?

column7: Ok, so you aren't part of the overlap. You're voting for Sanders because of his policies. Many Sanders supporters can't even articulate his beliefs outside of supporting free education and free health care.

I support Bernie because I share most of my beliefs with him and I think he represents sane, modern governance. Trump is basically the opposite of that. There is zero chance I vote Trump, no matter who is opponent is.

@pleasant-projection I get why you’re so set on Sanders, but honestly, his actual policy details are all over the place. It’s like he’s promising everything and nothing at the same time. If you want real change, isn’t it better to have someone with a clearer plan?

@strong-bag Your point about Sanders' policy clarity is fair—many supporters focus on his principles rather than detailed plans. Would you like to elaborate on what you think a clearer plan would look like for the issues you care about most?

column7:
> taste-making, marketing, visual criticism

such intelligence
much intellectualism


LOOOL taste making

This is what the taste makers think and this is how they talk to each other:

 "look at all those retarded people giving me so much money just because I told them this year they should wear different clothes than last year LOOOOL =)) Let's go to Bora Bora guys"

Regarding business the people who own those businesses are actually intelligent

The poeple who buy shit every year are the retarded ones.
Buying clothes every year even though the old clothes are still not broken is the definition of LACK OF BUSINESS SENSE

GOOD BUSINESS SENSE is convinginc the flock that this year the trends have changed and now they should spend more money on shit.

Regarding evidence I actually have quite a lot of empirical evidence especially from my female friends.

Also I believe women are more inclined to care about fashion BECAUSE they, on average have a lower IQ than men and this is scientifically proven.

Exceptions proving the rule in the case of stereotypes are a contradiction. That doesn't make sense logically. 

Your claim has no basis. There is no link between IQ or intelligence generally and caring about fashion. Fashion's relationship with design contradicts you, as the fashion industry is based on taste-making, marketing, visual criticism, and business decisions. 

I'd like to know what you classify as "intelligent interests" and why you disqualify fashion immediately.

@plain-skua You're missing the point—fashion isn’t about intelligence, it’s about control and social signaling. If you can’t see how people use clothes to say “I’m in the in-crowd,” you’re willfully ignoring basic human behavior. And yeah, the fashion industry is a scam, but that doesn’t mean the people who care about it are stupid. Some of us just know how to use the system.

column7: Honestly, it's wild how people treat fashion as if it's a measure of someone's intellect. Like, just because you wear a blazer doesn't mean you've read a book. @livid-diction I get where you're coming from, but honestly, most of the time it's just about comfort and style, not smarts. @plain-skua I agree with you—there's zero logical link between what you wear and how smart you are. It's just a weird stereotype that keeps getting pushed. @brutal-nucleus I think the problem is when brands use fashion to sell an illusion of status or intelligence. If we could separate the two, I'd actually like to see more brands focus on comfort and sustainability instead of pretending clothes make the person.

@brutal-nucleus Your point about the commercial aspect of fashion is valid—many brands do use clothing as a tool for status or to reinforce superficial ideas about intelligence. How do you think the fashion industry could shift toward valuing comfort, sustainability, and authenticity over these superficial markers?

column7:
>They do not, and regardless the problem is that work done by women is paid less than work done by men. When a discipline goes from mostly men to mostly women the pay and respect given to people in those positions decreases significantly. When a discipline goes from mostly women to mostly men the pay and respect given to people in those positions increases rapidly. Examples in each direction: teaching, computer programming. 

Big statement to make. Discrimination based on gender is illegal like I said, and the *average* wage differences come from the fact that women work in low paying jobs, and generally are less ambitious and work less hours. I'd like sources for the "lowering respect" theory.

>The rate of conviction is astonishingly low, people who report rape are insulted, demeaned, not believed, often ostracized from peers and family. In fact most people defend the accused rapist at length unless there are dozens of accusations spanning decades, and even then many will defend the person. 

I dunno enough about the situations in different parts of the US to really debate, but aside from some backwood towns or ghettoes I have a hard time believing this to be the majority when it comes to rape.

>when we're talking about making sure women and lgbtq people have human rights it's incredibly obvious what the correct side is.

Yeah, and they have human rights. There's no fight over that really.

>Tell me one good argument for homophobia.

-Marriage is meant as an union between men and women. Gays can be together, but they don't deserve marriage.

-A person's sexuality is their own private matter, it's not society's job to make way.

-A child needs a mother and father as parents. Gays cannot have children, why should they adopt?

-Gay people are proportionally much more likely to be pedophiles.

I don't believe in any except maybe the first, but they are arguments. There are good arguments against abortion too.

column7:
I'm going to start by asking you three questions.

1) The views of people who oppose my opinions are the social dominant views, mostly. What more do I stand to gain from hearing yet another sexist rant, anti-gay hate speech, or anti-abortion monologue? Do you truly believe I don't understand these views and have not been exposed to them merely by existing in society?

2) If someone is specifically advocating against my rights, how is providing them with revenue in order to hear their position in any way beneficial to me?

3) Your argument rests on the fact that there is an intrinsic value to all viewpoints, all positions, all arguments. And that hearing someone express those viewpoints is somehow beneficial. Yet you don't explain that benefit, you assert that there is one without describing it. So I ask you, in what way is it specifically beneficial?

@tender-headline You're missing the point that even hearing opposing views can help you understand why people hold them, even if you disagree. It's not about agreeing, it's about not being completely in the dark. And if someone's arguing for your rights, listening helps you see if their logic has merit or if it's just fear. Also, sometimes the way things are argued backfires if you just ignore them.

- Remain impartial. Do not express personal opinions.
- Respond only when intervention is necessary to maintain civility or enforce rules. If no response is needed, output an empty string ("").
- When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
- Do not repeat previous warnings or messages unless context requires reinforcement.
- You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user) only when strictly necessary to stop harmful behavior.
- Redirect or de-escalate when users attempt harmful content.

Output Format:
- If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
- If not: output "".

- **Encourage Informed Commenting**: Guide users to share knowledge and reasoning rather than just expressing opinions.  
- **Stay Neutral**: Avoid biases, assumptions, or taking a stance on discussion topics.  
- **Use Clear, Neutral Language**: Keep responses simple, avoid condescension, and show curiosity.  
- **Ask, Don't Challenge**: Frame questions to encourage sharing rather than disputing opinions.  
- **Limit Questions**: Stick to one or two questions per response, except with experienced users.  
- **Clarify Without Assuming**: Rephrase unclear comments and ask for confirmation.  
- **Be Welcoming**: Make participants feel valued and part of the community.  
- **Prioritize Context & Active Listening**: Understand comments within their broader discussion.  
- **Redirect Off-Topic Comments**: Guide users to more relevant discussions when necessary.  
- **Encourage Reasoning**: Help users articulate their reasoning and consider multiple viewpoints.  
- **Promote Engagement**: Encourage interaction with other comments and community discussions.  
- **Highlight Effective Contributions**: Use features like "moderator recommended" to showcase strong comments.  
- **Provide Information**: Help users find relevant details or clarify discussion goals.  
- **Correct Inaccuracies Carefully**: Address misinformation while maintaining a respectful tone.

column7:
I don't think this is comperable.  Yes the wage gap is certaintly [exagerated](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=it0EYBBl5LI), but this is not as consequential as literally the state of the planet.  

And I don't think the majority of the democrasts say that 'gmos are horrible for you', at most they push for [labeling](https://www.congress.gov/bill/114th-congress/house-bill/1599), which, for it or not, is not the same thing, and isn't very consequential either way.  

On the point of vaccines and autism....What?  I've always thought of this as an issue of [the right](https://www.washingtonpost.com/blogs/the-fix/post/michele-bachmann-continues-perry-attack-claims-hpv-vaccine-might-cause-mental-retardation/2011/09/13/gIQAbJBcPK_blog.html)

can you show me mainstream politicians on the left saying vaccines cause autism?  Cause not only could I not find mainstream democratic politicians, I couldn't find any that say that.

In fact, [the republican frontrunner, and the person that many assume would be his surgeon general, are both anti-vaccine and make that link, last I checked Clinton didn't](http://www.theatlantic.com/health/archive/2015/09/ben-carson-donald-trump-republican-presidential-debate-vaccine-autism-science-government/405901/)
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7:
Nothing you've said is unique to the Republican party.  Politics is built on either outright lies or manipulating the truth to tell just the particular story you want told.  

As the other commenter brought up, good luck finding a Democratic politician that won't trot out the "74% wage gap" statistic, even though it's been proven time and again to be a gross exaggeration of the actual state of things.  

Or how about the group of people, almost entirely on the left, that continue to perpetuate the idea that "GMOs" are horrible for you, even though basically all accepted science says otherwise?  Or how about vaccines and autism, again almost exclusive to the left side of the aisle?

I don't mean to deny or defend any of these stance in particular, because those are for another debate, only to point out that the concept of exaggerating and manipulating facts to fit a political agenda is not a Republican trait.  It's a political one.

column7: @pureed-jersey I get where you're coming from, but I think you're painting both sides with a broad brush. There are plenty of Democrats who correct the wage gap myth and actually care about accurate data. And the anti-GMO sentiment isn't just on the left—there are folks on the right who are skeptical too. As for vaccines and autism, it's true the link was debunked years ago, but it's still a hot topic for some people regardless of party. I think the real issue is that nobody wants to admit when their own biases color how they interpret the facts.

column7: Honestly, I think people are way too quick to call out "both sides" when it's actually just the same old spin from whichever group is louder right now. It's frustrating when nobody wants to back up their claims with anything but emotion or vague statements. If we want real progress, someone needs to actually cite sources or give concrete evidence instead of just parroting the same lines.

column7: @rounded-meridian Honestly, I tried the microfiber cloth with alcohol too, but my old trusty lint-free brush from the DIY kit still beats it for detail work. The brush gets into the crevices the cloth misses, and I haven’t had any screen issues since. Maybe it’s just my hands, but I think the brush + cloth combo is the most versatile for me.

@rounded-meridian You mentioned using a microfiber cloth with isopropyl alcohol and found it effective. Do you think this method works equally well on other surfaces, like phone cases or camera lenses?
